lead: Moving to Newmarket means settling into the global home of horse racing, a town where the rhythm of the gallops still shapes daily life. Sitting right on the Suffolk and Cambridgeshire border under the CB8 postcode, it manages to feel both like a busy market town and a close-knit community built around the studs and stables. Since 2005 our family-run crews have helped people move in and out of Newmarket, and we have grown rather fond of the place. Here is our honest local guide to making the move a smooth one.

sections.h: Getting to Know Newmarket Before You Move
sections.body:
Newmarket is unlike anywhere else we cover. The town wears its racing heritage openly, from the Jockey Club at its heart to the wide expanse of training gallops that wrap around the edges of town. If you have never lived here before, the first thing that strikes you is how the horses are woven into ordinary life. String after string of racehorses crosses the roads at quiet hours of the morning, and the town has its own crossings and customs built around them. It is a genuinely special thing to live alongside.

Beyond the racing, Newmarket is a working Suffolk town with a proper high street, schools, shops and a strong sense of its own identity. It sits close enough to Cambridge to make commuting practical, yet keeps the feel of somewhere smaller and friendlier. Many of the people we move here are drawn by exactly that balance: a place with real character and history that is still within easy reach of the city for work.

The border location matters too. Newmarket straddles the line between Suffolk and Cambridgeshire, which occasionally confuses newcomers when it comes to councils, services and catchment areas. It is worth checking which authority covers your new address early on, as it affects everything from bin collections to school applications. We always mention this to families moving in, because it is the sort of small detail that is easy to overlook in the rush of completion.

sections.h: Choosing the Right Part of Town
sections.body:
Newmarket spreads out around its centre with a real mix of property types, and where you settle depends a great deal on what you are after. Closer to the centre you find handsome older terraces and town houses, many with a history tied to the racing trade. These streets put you within walking distance of the shops and the High Street, which suits people who like to be in the thick of things and would rather not rely on the car for everyday errands.

Towards the edges of town the housing opens up into newer developments and quieter residential roads, often with more parking and a little more garden. These areas tend to appeal to growing families who want space without losing the convenience of the town. The studs and training establishments dominate the surrounding countryside, so a good deal of land around Newmarket is given over to horses rather than houses, which keeps the green setting that residents value so highly.

When we carry out a survey before a move, we always take a careful look at access. Some of the older central streets are narrow, with parking restrictions and limited room for a large removals lorry. Knowing this in advance lets us plan the right vehicle and, where needed, arrange to park sensibly so the day runs without a hitch. The newer estates are usually more straightforward, but it never hurts to think it through early.

sections.h: Living Alongside the Gallops and Studs
sections.body:
One thing that genuinely sets Newmarket apart is daily life around the gallops. The training grounds are active from very early in the morning, and horses have right of way across the dedicated crossings. If you are not used to it, the sight of a long string of thoroughbreds crossing the road as you head out can be startling at first, but it quickly becomes one of the things you love about the place. There is nothing else quite like it anywhere in the country.

For anyone working in the racing industry, the appeal is obvious, and a great many of our Newmarket moves involve people relocating for jobs at the studs, yards and the Jockey Club. The town is built to support that world, with everything from specialist veterinary services to equine suppliers close at hand. Even if you have no connection to racing yourself, you soon find that the calendar of the racing year shapes the town's busiest and quietest spells.

It does pay to factor the early-morning activity into where you choose to live, especially if you are a light sleeper. Properties right beside the main gallops or training yards can be lively from before dawn. We are not saying it is a problem, far from it, but it is the kind of local knowledge worth having before you commit. The town has a gentle, purposeful energy to it that most people grow to treasure.

Newmarket's position on the Suffolk and Cambridgeshire border makes it a practical base for commuters. Cambridge is close enough to reach comfortably, which opens up a wide range of work without having to live in the city itself. Plenty of the people we move to Newmarket are doing exactly that, swapping a cramped Cambridge address for more space and character a short journey out, while keeping the city within easy reach.

Within the town itself, much of daily life is walkable, particularly if you settle near the centre. The High Street covers most everyday needs, and the town is compact enough that you are rarely far from the shops. For longer journeys the road links are good, connecting Newmarket out towards the wider region and the motorway network beyond. Families tell us they appreciate not feeling cut off, which can be a worry when moving to a smaller town.

Public transport and the train connection give you further options, and many residents find they use the car less than they expected once they have settled in. As with any move, our advice is to spend a little time learning your new routes before the big day, so the first week feels less like starting from scratch. We are always happy to talk through what we have learned about getting around the town over the years.

sections.h: What to Sort Before and After the Move
sections.body:
The practical side of moving to Newmarket is much like anywhere else, with one local quirk: the border between Suffolk and Cambridgeshire. Make sure you know which council covers your new home, as it determines your bin collections, council tax band and school catchments. Newcomers occasionally get caught out here, registering with the wrong authority and having to untangle it later. A quick check at the start saves a good deal of bother.

Beyond that, the usual admin applies. Redirect your post, update your address with the bank, the DVLA, your insurers and your GP, and arrange your utilities at both ends. Take dated photographs of the meters on the day so you are never billed for someone else's usage. If you have children, get the school transfers under way early, particularly given the catchment quirks that come with the town's border position.

When it comes to the move itself, an early survey is the key to a calm day. It lets us judge the volume, spot any access challenges on the narrower central streets, and bring the right lorry and crew. Everything we move is fully covered, and our crews have years of experience navigating Newmarket's particular layout. Get the planning done in good time and the move into your new Suffolk-border home becomes something to look forward to rather than dread.

faqs: Newmarket falls under the CB8 postcode. Because the town sits right on the Suffolk and Cambridgeshire border, it is worth checking which council actually covers your specific address, as that affects bin collections, council tax and school catchments.
Is Newmarket a good place to live if I work in Cambridge?

Yes, many of the people we move to Newmarket commute to Cambridge. The town's position on the border keeps the city within easy reach while giving you more space and character than a city address, which is exactly why it appeals to so many commuters.
What is it like living near the gallops in Newmarket?

Living near Newmarket's gallops means early-morning activity, with strings of racehorses crossing the roads at quiet hours. Most residents grow to love it, but if you are a light sleeper it is worth thinking about how close to the main training grounds you settle.
Are Newmarket's central streets difficult for a removals lorry?

Some of Newmarket's older central streets are narrow with parking restrictions, which is why we always survey before a move. Knowing the access in advance lets us bring the right vehicle and park sensibly, so the day runs smoothly whatever part of town you are moving to.
